questions.prompt: A psychologist explains that behaviours such as rule-breaking, aggression, and defiance exist on a spectrum rather than as completely separate categories. Which perspective on conduct problems does this reflect?
A child frequently argues with adults, deliberately annoys others, and refuses to follow rules but does not engage in severe aggression or property destruction. Which disorder best fits this pattern?
A teenager repeatedly engages in serious behaviors such as vandalism, stealing, physical aggression, and cruelty toward animals. Which disorder is most consistent with this pattern?

A child frequently interprets neutral actions by peers as intentionally hostile. For example, when accidentally bumped in the hallway, the child assumes the other student meant to harm them. Which cognitive bias best explains this interpretation?

A psychologist suggests that a youth’s aggressive behavior may reflect trauma exposure, attachment insecurity, and emotional dysregulation rather than simply a rule-breaking personality. Which framework does this reflect?

A clinician labels a child as having conduct disorder, and teachers subsequently begin expecting the child to behave aggressively. Over time the child begins acting more aggressively. Which phenomenon does this example illustrate?
Which developmental pathway describes individuals who show persistent antisocial behavior beginning early in childhood and continuing into adulthood?
Research indicates that most individuals who engage in antisocial behavior do so primarily during which developmental period?
A child appears emotionally detached, rejects help from caregivers, and acts aggressively when independence feels threatened. Which attachment pattern may underlie this presentation?
A child alternates between clinging to caregivers and expressing anger toward them, showing intense emotional reactions when they feel rejected. Which attachment pattern best fits this description?
A child displays chaotic and contradictory behaviors toward caregivers who are both a source of comfort and fear. Which attachment pattern does this most likely represent?
Which underlying process is commonly shared across many disruptive behavior disorders according to the trans-diagnostic perspective?
A psychologist explains that diagnosis of disruptive behavior disorders often focuses on observable behavior rather than underlying mechanisms. Which critique of diagnostic systems does this reflect?
Research shows that Black and Latinx youth are more likely to receive diagnoses of ODD or conduct disorder compared to ADHD. Which issue does this illustrate?

A psychologist suggests exploring environmental factors such as poverty, trauma, and social context when evaluating disruptive behaviors rather than focusing only on individual pathology. Which conceptual model does this align with?
Which goal best reflects the philosophy of restorative justice when responding to youth offending behavior?
Restorative justice aligns with adolescent neurodevelopment research because adolescents are still developing which brain system responsible for planning and impulse control?
Which factor is most strongly associated with increased risk of disruptive behavior disorders?
Callous-unemotional traits in conduct disorder are most associated with which behavioral pattern?
Which concept best explains why behaviors that seem defiant in one context may actually reflect developmentally typical behavior in another?
